note: Five experiments (A-E) against DBOS Transact 2.27.0 on the SQLite default backend. The durable claims are the RECOVERY SEMANTICS (which steps replay vs re-run) and the version-trap / durability-tax SHAPE. Absolute recovery latencies are point observations (machine-dependent) and reproduced within the same order of magnitude on the 2026-07-19 re-run.

findings.claim: Hard-killing the process mid-step and merely relaunching resumes the workflow to SUCCESS.
findings.detail: step3 hard-crashes on its first attempt (os._exit(42), before its output is checkpointed). On relaunch in a NEW process, step1/step2 are NOT re-executed (their outputs are replayed from the DB) and step3 is re-executed, then step4/step5 run to completion. Final status=SUCCESS, result 'result-1,...,result-5'. No queue, no server — pip install + decorators + SQLite default only.

findings.claim: The unit of at-least-once is the STEP: a completed (checkpointed) step is never re-run; only a step that died before recording is.
findings.detail: Crash AFTER step3 completes but BEFORE step4 starts. On relaunch, step3 is NOT re-executed (ledger shows no second step3 line) and the workflow continues from step4. Contrast with experiment A where step3 died mid-execution and WAS re-run. Implication: a side effect inside a step (e.g. a payment API call) can run more than once across a crash — idempotency keys remain the caller's responsibility. 'Exactly-once' holds for the workflow only if each step is idempotent.

findings.claim: The 'magic' is a database, not a runtime trick: recovery is replay from ~10 tables.
findings.detail: The DBOS system db (dbos-firsthand.sqlite, 164KB) contains 10 tables including workflow_status (status, name, recovery_attempts, application_version) and operation_outputs (per-step function_id order + serialized output). On recovery, if a step's function_id already has a recorded output, DBOS returns that output WITHOUT calling the function (replay); otherwise it executes and records. workflow_status for kill-test-1 shows status=SUCCESS, recovery_attempts=2 after the crash+resume cycle.

findings.claim: If the code (app version) changes, recovery STOPS — a pending workflow is not handed to a process running a different version.
findings.detail: Crash under APP_VERSION=alpha. Relaunch under APP_VERSION=beta: the workflow stays PENDING for the full 30s wait window, with a warning that 'beta' is not the latest version. Relaunch under APP_VERSION=alpha (matching): immediate recovery to SUCCESS. Reason: replay assumes the same code calls the same steps in the same order, so DBOS will not give a version-mismatched process a pending workflow (default). Operational consequence: 'just relaunch and it continues' is true FOR THE SAME CODE VERSION; a rolling deploy that changes the code mid-flight can orphan in-flight workflows (they need an old-version executor, or a fork/patch to migrate).

findings.claim: Durability is not free: each no-op durable step costs ~2.3ms on local SQLite (the per-step checkpoint write).
findings.detail: A 200-no-op-step workflow takes ~0.46s vs ~9e-06s for the equivalent plain function calls -> ~2.3ms per step, all attributable to the checkpoint write. Do not wrap tight-loop inner calls as steps. A remote Postgres backend would add network RTT on top and be larger (cited, NOT measured here).

metrics.label: Relaunch -> workflow SUCCESS after a mid-flight hard kill (same app version)
metrics.note: Machine-dependent (SQLite on local disk). The DURABLE claim is that relaunch alone recovers; the seconds are an observation, not a portable number.

metrics.label: Per-step cost of a no-op durable step (200-step workflow, local SQLite)
metrics.note: vs ~9e-06s total for 200 plain calls. The signal is 'checkpointing has a real per-step cost, order 1-10ms on local SQLite', not the exact 2.3.

metrics.label: Recovery under a MISMATCHED app version
metrics.value: PENDING (no recovery) for the full 30s wait; SUCCESS only under the matching version

evidence_ledgers.note: Verbatim append-only ledgers from the 2026-07-17 run (ground truth of real (re-)execution). 'EXECUTED' = the code actually ran; absence on replay = DBOS returned a recorded output without running.
crash_during: step1 EXECUTED / step2 EXECUTED / step3 EXECUTED
step3 EXECUTED (re-run) / step4 EXECUTED / step5 EXECUTED  -> SUCCESS
crash_between: KILLING PROCESS BETWEEN step3 AND step4
step4 EXECUTED / step5 EXECUTED  (step3 NOT re-run) -> SUCCESS
version_trap: --- resume launch app_version=beta   -> PENDING (no step re-runs)
--- resume launch app_version=alpha  -> step3/4/5 EXECUTED -> SUCCESS

explicitly_not_verified.note: Only the SQLite default backend was exercised. The production-recommended Postgres backend adds network RTT to every checkpoint; the durability tax there is cited as larger, NOT measured.
DBOS recovers a workflow WHEN a process relaunches. It does not resurrect the process itself — a supervisor (systemd / k8s / a human) must restart it. That layer was not part of this probe.
Single-process recovery only. Multi-executor / distributed recovery and queue semantics under contention were not tested.
Python SDK only. TS/Go/Java SDKs share the design but were not run.
